title: Heartbeat-failure self-shutdown sends SIGTERM but never escalates to SIGKILL

findingDetails.evidence: In startOwnerLeaseHeartbeat (537-551), on refresh failure the handler does `if (isChildRunning(childProcess)) childProcess.kill('SIGTERM');` (546) then synchronously `clearAllLeaseFiles(); process.exit(128);` (547-548). No waitForChildExit, no SIGKILL fallback. The advisor child handles SIGTERM via an async shutdownAdvisor(...).finally(()=>process.exit(0)) (advisor-server.ts:329-330), so it is mid-teardown when its launcher exits and is reparented to init with no escalation backstop.
findingDetails.why: This branch exists to 'preserve single ownership' after a successor launcher reclaims the owner lease, but the successor will spawn a fresh advisor child while this child is still alive. A child that is slow or hangs in shutdownAdvisor becomes an orphaned duplicate of the advisor server — the exact double-ownership the path is meant to prevent. Inconsistent with the signal-handler path (1192-1198) which waits 5000ms then SIGKILLs.
findingDetails.recommendation: Mirror the signal-handler escalation: after childProcess.kill('SIGTERM'), await waitForChildExit(childProcess, <grace>) and childProcess.kill('SIGKILL') if still running before process.exit(128); make the handler async or move the exit into the wait continuation.

title: Reap escalation kills a recorded PID with no executable/start-time verification (PID-reuse risk)

findingDetails.evidence: reapOwnerBeforeRespawn (406-435) issues process.kill(ownerPid,'SIGTERM') (417) and process.kill(ownerPid,'SIGKILL') (425) keyed only on processLiveness; same for the main() pendingBootstrapReapPid reap (1244). targetPid originates from a recorded childPid/ownerPid in a stale lease. The owner lease stores executablePath (332,379) but classifyOwnerLease (457-474) and the respawn reap never compare it.
findingDetails.why: If the OS recycled the recorded PID to an unrelated process between recording and reap, the launcher SIGKILLs an innocent process. It is the one kill-target on the escalation path with no guard stronger than bare PID liveness.
findingDetails.recommendation: Before SIGTERM/SIGKILL of a recorded PID, verify it is still the expected process (compare /proc or `ps` comm/executablePath against the lease's executablePath, or a recorded start-time), and treat a mismatch as already-dead rather than killing.
